无论你是待在原地还是外出，夏天都是放松和尝试新事物的好时机。所以我们请TED演讲者推荐最近滋养了他们的思想、精神和身体的播客、书籍、电视节目、电影和更多的东西(是的，你可以在下面找到橄榄奶酪面包食谱的链接)。
您可以使用此处的链接跳转到您最喜欢的类别。这份清单分为三个部分：“要听的东西(主要是播客)”；“要读的东西(主要是书)”；“要看的东西(主要是电视节目和电影)”。或者，你可以滚动浏览并停留在任何吸引你注意力或兴趣的地方。
请尽情享受，我们希望这些建议能像对TED演讲者一样给您带来营养。
这是一个由迈克尔·哈克主持的鲍勃·迪伦初级播客，这个播客是一位变形金刚的奇妙生活和音乐的个人指南，他是一位改变了自己和周围文化的音乐家。随着我年龄的增长，他的歌有了新的含义--“再见是个太好的词…。-尤里·阿隆(Uri Alon)，TED对话：新冠肺炎结束封锁并重新开放经济的“退出”战略。
平衡黑人女孩播客主持人莱斯·阿尔弗雷德的声音立刻让我平静下来。她通常以呼吸或深呼吸开始播客。她讲述了日常工作与生活平衡的典型斗争，以及世界上正在发生的影响美国黑人的事情。她的播客滋养了我的思想和灵魂。我停下来，只听--不要一心多用。我在她呼吸的时候呼吸，我倾听，我整天都在想我从播客中学到的小金块。-阿纳斯塔西亚·彭赖特(Anastasia Penright)，TED Talk：从工作中的戏剧中解脱出来的5个步骤。
Om播客里的黑人女孩这是我最喜欢的播客！主持人劳伦·阿什为黑人女性创造了一种既有营养又有力量的体验。从正念运动到子宫智慧，这部剧的主题总是及时和相关的。劳伦平静的声音是一个额外的奖励，每次我听的时候都会把我带回中心。奥姆的黑人女孩现在是我健康对话的首选。-Ebony Roberts，TED Talk：如何作为盟友共同为人父母-而不是对手。
将幸福带入生活播客Denise Quinlan博士的爱尔兰轻快节奏和百科全书式的幸福知识使她成为我最喜欢的播客，当谈到弹性资源和建立我们个人和集体的福祉时，她是我最喜欢的播客。她接待全球有价值的客人，总是设法问他们所有正确的问题，而不会打断他们的流动。-露西·霍恩(Lucy Hone)，TED Talk：坚韧不拔的人的3个秘密。
密码切换播客在我们国家一次又一次与种族不公正的遗产作斗争之际，NPR的密码切换播客是由有色人种作家制作的播客，内容是关于种族和歧视的当代和历史问题，以及我们作为一个国家如何找到希望，朝着不同和更好的方向前进。-劳拉·赖特(Laura Wright)，TED-ED课程：为什么你要读阿伦哈蒂·罗伊(Arundhati Roy)的“小事之神”？
幸福实验室播客这个播客由Laurie Santos博士主持(TED Talk：一个和我们一样非理性的猴子经济)，带你了解最新的幸福研究，并提供有洞察力的轶事。它会让你每天都有不同的感觉，特别是在这个起伏不定的时候。-TED Talk的Catie Cuan：教机器人如何跳舞。
美国奴隶史播客这个石板播客从多个角度详细概述了美国奴隶制的历史。主持人Jamelle Bouie和Rebecca Onion采访学者，探索学校课程中很少涉及的话题，让听众带着对美国历史的丰富了解离开。从一些第一批在后来的美国定居的非洲人的故事到非裔美国人经历解放的方式，这个系列提供了一个大学水平的理解，任何级别的学习者都可以接触到。当我们着眼于解决历史和当代的种族、正义、自由和平等问题时，这个系列应该要求所有美国人倾听，这些问题即使在这个非常时刻也对我们构成了挑战。-戴娜·贝瑞(Daina Berry)，TED-ED课程：旅居者真相的激动人心的演讲。
我是如何制作这个播客的这是一个很棒的播客，可以在你的业余时间收听。它由盖伊·拉兹(Guy Raz)主持，深入探讨创新者和企业家如何创建发起一场运动的公司的故事。如今一些伟大的公司并不是故意创办的。我们都可以欣赏一家成功的公司，但真正吸引人的是创新者、企业家和理想主义者的个人故事。此外，这对我来说是一个很好的提醒，让我真正欣赏我自己的故事，因为谁知道它会带来什么变化呢？--TED Talk的Rob Cooke：工作压力的代价--以及如何减轻它。
如何在世界末日幸存这个播客，由活动家Adrienne Maree Brown和Aufall Brown主持，甚至超出了标题承诺的范围。在彼此的交谈中，以及与其他令人惊叹的活动家的交谈中，他们揭示了一个崩溃中的世界的方方面面，并展示了这些裂缝如何转化为复兴的机会。-伊法特·苏斯金德(Yifat Susskin)，TED Talk：在不确定的时期，像母亲一样思考。
Lady Don‘t Take No Podcast这个来自Black Lives Matter联合创始人艾丽西亚·加尔扎(Alicia Garza)的个人播客充满了笑声和爱，带来了对文化做出有意义贡献的嘉宾，同时也提振了我们的精神。-希瑟·C·麦基(Heather C.McGhee)，TED演讲：种族主义对每个人都有代价。
Librivox有声读物我非常感谢多年来我从Librivox得到的支持，Librivox是一个由慷慨的志愿者阅读的免费的公共领域有声读物的巨大图书馆。我经常不是根据作者而是根据读者选择我的书，我最喜欢的是已故的尼古拉斯·克利福德(Nicholas Clifford，1930-2019年)，他似乎天生就是为朗读亨利·詹姆斯(Henry James)而生的。我可以整天听克利福德和蔼可亲的音调，我从来没有想过詹姆斯臭名昭著的挑衅性“晚期风格”会像这些录音中那样清晰、自然和人性化。伊丽莎白·克莱特读内拉·拉森的作品也很出色。-萨沙·莫雷尔(Sascha Morrell)，TED-ED课程：你为什么要读《白鲸》？
传说播客我是一个历史保护书呆子，住在据说闹鬼的房子里，养着一只可怕的黑猫，我有几个朋友在老美术馆和画廊工作，或者从事建筑修复工作。我们都有很棒的故事。给我们一些葡萄酒或威士忌，我们都会在一个关于我们如此热爱的摇摇欲坠的老建筑里发生的令人毛骨悚然的故事中度过一个晚上。在禁闭期间，我非常怀念这些夜晚。但由亚伦·曼克(Aaron Mahnke)主持的Lore是一个合适的替代品。每一集都通过一系列松散联系的故事讲述“历史的阴暗面”，但它们的讲述方式并不像许多现代恐怖电影那样令人震惊或恶心，而是为了编造一个伟大的故事。篝火可选。-卡罗琳·麦卡锡(Caroline McCarthy)，TED Talk：广告是如何分裂我们的。
肖恩·卡罗尔(Sean Carroll)的Mindscape播客这个播客是我去满足人们对科学、艺术、流行文化、历史、哲学等领域新的、迷人的想法和神秘事物的渴望的地方。肖恩·卡罗尔(Sean Carroll)采访了意想不到的领域的思想家和实干家，从扑克心理学和古代自动机到量子物理和流行病，他深思熟虑的问题使每一集都充满了对智力的持久滋养。听完心境课程后，你总会觉得自己更聪明！-阿德里安·市长，TED-ED课程：亚马逊人真的存在吗？
Moth播客我们都分享着这段人类经历，要想真正理解它，带着同理心和爱，你只需听一个人用他们自己的话讲述自己的故事。就像飞蛾对火焰一样，我们是为了在一起的温暖而来的。光是听这个播客我就学到了很多，而且我总是想要更多。-Lee Thomas，TED演讲：我如何帮助人们了解白癜风。
讲故事是建立同理心的有力方式。我会散步，听飞蛾的歌声，经常在听这些故事的时候哭泣，因为这些故事所表达的是人性。我喜欢分享的生活经历的广度-从小经历到大经历，以及来自各行各业的人。-莎拉·琼斯(Sara Jones)，TED Talk：收回我作为跨种族被领养人的声音。
“减速”播客“减速”正如它的名字所暗示的那样--它帮助你停下来，喘口气。每集都很短(只有5分钟！)。而且很简单。诗人特蕾西·K·史密斯从反思她生活中最近发生的事情开始，然后用她平静、沉思的声音朗读了一首诗。我发现这是一个休息的好方法，感受诗歌唤起的广泛的情感和生动的想象力，并发现新的作家。-Ananya Grover，TED Talk：一场争取时期积极的运动。
“作家之声”播客和“小说播客”文学播客是我在匆忙奔波或陷入无脑任务时精神营养的主要来源，其中两个最受欢迎的播客是来自“纽约客”杂志的“作家之声”和“小说播客”。前者的特色是最近几期的小说，由作者自己阅读，而后者的特色是作家们从过去的几期中选择故事朗读，并与《纽约客》的小说编辑黛博拉·特雷斯曼(Deborah Treisman)讨论。我喜欢在“作家之声”中收听某位作家的新作品，同时也喜欢在小说播客中阅读该作者的另一部作品。作为开胃菜，试试安妮·恩赖特(Anne Enright)为“作家之声”朗读她的故事“夜泳”(Night Swim)，然后在她为小说播客(Fiction Podcast)阅读和讨论约翰·奇弗(John Cheever)的“游泳者”时将其一扫而光。-萨沙·莫雷尔(Sascha Morrell)，TED-ED课程：你为什么要读《白鲸》？
TouréShow播客节目中扎迪·史密斯接受采访的那一集记者托雷指出，扎迪·史密斯是最善于让聪明看起来真的很酷的人。当我读到扎迪关于快乐和快乐之间重要区别的文章时，我就迷上了她。与她的这次采访让我觉得可以审问自己的想法，她让我想要多读多写。她才华横溢，图埃也才华横溢，他们谈论Jay-Z、写作、家庭、妮娜·西蒙妮(Nina Simone)，以及用紧迫的正直写作所需的勇气。在图埃位于布鲁克林的公寓里，他们两人共同对审美风险感到兴奋，这场对话让人感觉时间被搁置了。-莎拉·桑福德(Sara Sanford)，TED演讲：如何在工作场所之外设计性别偏见。
吠陀世界观播客托姆·诺尔斯(Thom Knoles)是我的导师，也是吠陀冥想的卓越大师老师，他已经教会了全世界超过4万人冥想。他的播客充满了关于如何在追求人生道路的同时过着没有痛苦的生活的知识。无论你是否打坐，他的智慧话语和平静的声音都会让你安心。-TED Talk的Angel Chang：古代纺织品如何帮助未来。
“发生了什么”专辑这张及时的经典专辑充满了马文·盖伊千载难逢的嗓音，震撼的音调，有社会意识的歌词，以及精神上充满希望的沉思。它发行于1971年(我出生前一年)，令人惊讶的是，它听起来就像是为这一时刻写的。
“吉尔伽美什史诗”已有近5000年的历史，讲述的是美索不达米亚国王吉尔伽美什及其对永生秘密的探索，这是我们拥有的最早的人类故事。对于那些认为只有现代人才能解决棘手问题的人来说，这部史诗是一堂谦虚的课。读一读吉尔伽美什的故事，它永恒地描绘了人类的核心问题-正义、暴力、性、爱、友谊、权力、死亡-你将永远不会再以同样的方式看待古老的世界。-菲利普·弗里曼(Phillip Freeman)，TED-ED课程：古代凯尔特德鲁伊生活中的一天
豪尔赫·路易斯·博尔赫斯(Jorge Luis Borges)的短篇小说“巴别塔图书馆”(A Library Of Babel)(可以在这里找到)我们几乎不可能理解我们的宇宙到底有多大，但阿根廷神话作家豪尔赫·路易斯·博尔赫斯(Jorge Luis Borges)试了试。在他最不同寻常的故事之一“巴别塔图书馆”中，他梦想出一个宇宙大小的图书馆。博尔赫斯在担任图书馆助理馆长期间，设想了一个“由不确定甚至可能是无限数量的六边形画廊组成的图书馆，其间有巨大的通风井，周围有非常低的栏杆。”就像宇宙一样，这种奇妙的类似埃舍尔的结构既被生动地描述，又不可能被想象-一个巨大的宇宙-作为图书馆，被认为包含了基本书籍结构的所有可能的410页排列，足以表达每一部作品的某个版本，以及一切可能的东西。在有限的世界里，宇宙观不是奢侈品，而是必需品。向公众传达我们宇宙的浩瀚是天文学家和作家面临的真正挑战。-恩里科·拉米雷斯-鲁伊斯(Enrico Ramirez-Ruiz)，TED Talk：你的身体是在壮观的明星死亡中锻造的。
奥克塔维亚·巴特勒的“播种者的寓言”奥克塔维亚·巴特勒是如此超前于她的时代。由我的好朋友Shamell Bell博士推荐给我，Octavia为读者创建的世界令人毛骨悚然地感觉像是我们目前面临的2020年世界的一个严肃的伏笔。-乔尔·利昂(Joel Leon)，TED Talk：共同育儿的美丽而艰苦的工作。
保罗·科埃略(Paulo Coelho)的“炼金术士”这是我在任何时候都绝对最喜欢读的寓言之一，因为无论你生活在哪里，它都具有相关性。它是永恒的。对我来说，这是一本书，它有能力让任何人受到启发，也可以让那些没有灵感的人反思，希望这能带来灵感。它不断地帮助我重塑我对生活中的考验和考验的看法，认为这是通往自我发现的必要课程。一本关于弹性、反思和成长的奇妙的书。-TED Talk的罗布·库克(Rob Cooke)：工作压力的成本-以及如何减轻压力。
克里斯汀·汉娜(Kristin Hannah)的“夜莺”这部小说让我在今年最艰难的禁闭时期有了一种透视感，提醒我，无论我们认为情况有多糟糕，总会有一些人忍受得更多。-露西·霍恩(Lucy Hone)，TED Talk：坚韧不拔的人的3个秘密。
“美国的日出：乔伊·哈霍的诗”这本美丽的诗集从第一位美国本土桂冠诗人的角度描绘了我们作为美国人是谁，也描绘了我们可能成为的人。-伊法特·苏斯金德(Yifat Susskin)，TED Talk：在不确定的时期，像母亲一样思考。
托夫·詹森的“夏日之书”特别是因为我住在城市里，我喜欢寻找能把我带回大自然的书。我刚开始做这件事，我很喜欢。它讲述了苏菲亚和索菲亚的祖母在芬兰湾一个未被破坏的小岛上度过夏天的故事，索菲亚是一个6岁的女孩，她意识到了自己的存在，而她的祖母即将离开她的生命尽头。这部电影讲述了索菲亚和索菲亚的祖母在芬兰湾一个未被破坏的小岛上度过夏天的故事。-尚特尔·马丁，TED Talk：绘画如何让你自由。
伊丽莎白·科斯托娃的“历史学家”这部小说对弗拉德·特普斯(又名德古拉伯爵)的生活进行了真正伟大而可怕的讲述。它的情节新颖而引人入胜。这部集历史、科幻小说和冒险于一身的历史学家不适合胆小的人，但它比许多现代恐怖故事中血淋淋的胡说八道要多得多。如果邪恶就像这个故事一样简单明了呢？生活会变得更容易吗？--乔治·C·本杰明，TED对话：对抗流行病的秘密武器。
“和平战士之路：一本改变生活的书”丹·米尔曼著这本书已经伴随我很多很多年了，这是我读过的第一本书，它迫使我注意到心灵可以成为你最大的盟友或敌人。它还告诉我要避免过于执着于物质和成就，单靠成就不会带来生活的目标感和成就感，并让我对可能被我视为理所当然的简单事情进行盘点。归根结底，这些往往会带来最大的价值。鉴于当今世界的演变如此剧烈，我打赌这是一本现在每个人都可以受益的书。-TED Talk的罗布·库克(Rob Cooke)：工作压力的成本-以及如何减轻压力
理查德·鲍尔斯(Richard Power)的这部小说获得了普利策奖(Pulitzer Award)，这部巨著讲述了一群最初不知情的活动家为拯救太平洋西北部的古老红杉而战的故事。我教环境文学，我读和教的很多东西可能会非常令人沮丧和世界末日。相比之下，这部关于环境破坏的小说令人奇怪地充满希望，尽管如此，它还是给读者留下了一些舒适感。-劳拉·赖特(Laura Wright)，TED-ED课程：为什么你要读阿伦哈蒂·罗伊(Arundhati Roy)的《小事之神》？
布莱尔·里士满的“Lithia三部曲”(“喘不过气来了”、“幽灵跑步者”、“最后一英里”)我推荐这部YA连续剧，讲述的是一个名叫凯特·琼斯的19岁长跑运动员和吸血鬼的故事。哦，他们是纯素食的吸血鬼。-劳拉·赖特(Laura Wright)，TED-ED课程：为什么你要读阿伦哈蒂·罗伊(Arundhati Roy)的《小事之神》？
J.D.塞林格的“麦田里的守望者”我第一次读这本小说是在我十几岁的时候，它成功地捕捉到了孤立感、孤独感和一种不适应的普遍感觉。当时，霍尔登似乎是一个不知不觉但恰如其分的同志，他帮助确立了内部动荡时期是真实的、活着的人类经历，我们都必须经历和努力。我有时仍然会想起这个家伙，和世界上其他的霍尔顿一样，想知道他们过得怎么样。-法国维拉尔塔，TED Talk：菲律宾性别不稳定的历史。
理查德·桑福德的“星环”全面披露：我与作者有亲戚关系，但即使这本小说不是由家人写的，我也会觉得它很有滋养。星环是我们需要的故事。它写于2012年，是一部未来主义小说，现在感觉真实得令人不安。当随机和例行公事的暴力分裂美国时，主人公得到了一个愿景。在像余烬一样燃烧的美国城镇之外，他建造了一个安全的避风港：一个经典的免下车电影院，一堵装饰着云母、玻璃和镜子碎片的圆形墙壁。站在我们这个时代的力量面前，桑福德在这个国家的混乱中照亮了一条道路，让我们看到了我们许多人渴望的东西：共享空间；彼此之间的安全；逃亡的心灵找到了休息的地方。-莎拉·桑福德(Sara Sanford)，TED演讲：如何在工作场所之外设计性别偏见。
J·R·R·托尔金的“指环王”，当我的生活发生剧变或变化时，我发现自己在重读“指环王”。当我还是个孩子的时候，我的父母给我读了这本书，这和托尔金华丽的写作和奇幻的土地，善恶的共鸣结合在一起，帮助我找到了中心。-埃里克·桑纳鲁德(Eric Sannerud)，TED Talk：没有农民，你就会挨饿，赤身裸体，清醒。
“莫斯科绅士”作者：阿莫尔·陶尔斯(Amor Towles)：我本打算去俄罗斯旅行--这是我第一次来俄罗斯--但后来大流行爆发了。所以，我凑合着看了一本小说，把我带到了大都会酒店(不，我实际上并不打算住在那里)。虽然这是对酒店内部的极好描绘，但它更好地探索了一系列社会问题，包括社会孤立(对于许多大流行前的人来说，这是一个不幸的现实，在大流行期间，对更多的人来说，这是一个不幸的现实)和阶级问题。-TED Talk的Vinay Shandal：投资者如何有意识地加大热情，使公司发生变化。
肯尼亚作家Ngugi WA Thiong‘o的“乌鸦巫师”是一部关于虚构的非洲国家的政治喜剧讽刺作品，讲述的是一个虚构的非洲国家，它的狂妄自大的统治者，以及迎合他每一个白痴奇想的花言巧语。从口头的高度表演性的语言基库尤语翻译过来，叙事内容宽泛，神奇，杂乱无章-总共700多页。我大约有五年没教过它了，但我现在一直在想。
.
note_en:
Whether you’re staying put or going away, summer is a great time to relax and to try new things. So we asked TED speakers to recommend podcasts, books, TV shows, movies and more that have nourished their minds, spirits and bodies (yes, you’ll find a link to a recipe for olive-cheese loaf below) in recent times.
 You can use the links here to jump to your favorite category. The list is divided into three buckets:  “Things to listen to (primarily podcasts)”; “Things to read (basically books)”; and  “Things to watch (mainly TV shows and movies)”. Or, you can just scroll through and stop at anything that catches your attention or interest.
 Enjoy, and we hope these recommendations are as nourishing for you as they have been to TED’s speakers.
  A Bob Dylan Primer podcast Hosted by Michael Hacker, this podcast is a personal guide to the strange and wonderful life and music of a transformer, a musician who’s transformed himself and the culture around him. As I grow older, his songs take on new meanings — “  Goodbye’s too good a word … ” – Uri Alon, TED Conversation:  A COVID-19 “exit” strategy to end lockdown and reopen the economy
 Balanced Black Girl podcast  Host Les Alfred has a voice that immediately calms me down. She usually starts out the podcast with breathing or with just taking one big breath. She relates typical struggles of everyday work-life balance and about what’s happening in the world that impacts Black America. Her podcast nourishes my mind and soul. I stop and just listen — no multitasking. I breathe when she breathes, I listen, and I think about the little nuggets I learned from the podcast throughout the day. – Anastasia Penright, TED Talk:  5 steps to remove yourself from drama at work
 Black Girl In Om podcast  This is my new favorite podcast! Host Lauren Ash has created an experience for Black womxn that is both nourishing and empowering. From episodes on mindful movement to womb wisdom, the show’s topics are always timely and relevant. Lauren’s calming voice is an added bonus and brings me back to center every time I listen.  Black Girl in Om is now my go-to for wellness conversations. – Ebony Roberts, TED Talk:  How to co-parent as allies – not adversaries
 Bringing Wellbeing To Life podcast  Dr. Denise Quinlan’s Irish lilt and encyclopedic knowledge of wellbeing makes her my favorite podcaster when it comes to resilience resources and building our personal and collective well-being. She gets globally valued guests and always manages to ask them all the right questions, not interrupting their flow. – Lucy Hone, TED Talk:  3 secrets of resilient people
 Code Switch podcast  In the present moment as our nation grapples again and again with its legacy of racial injustice, NPR’s  Code Switch is a podcast produced by writers of color about contemporary and historic issues about race and discrimination, and how we, as a country, might find hope and move in a different and better direction. – Laura Wright, TED-Ed Lesson:  Why should you read “The God of Small Things” by Arundhati Roy?
 The Happiness Lab podcast This podcast, hosted by Dr. Laurie Santos (TED Talk:  A monkey economy as irrational as ours), takes you through the latest happiness research and offers insightful anecdotes. It will make you feel differently about your day to day, especially during these up-and-down times. – Catie Cuan, TED Talk:  Teaching robots how to dance
 The History of American Slavery podcast This Slate podcast provides a detailed overview of the history of American slavery from multiple perspectives. Hosts Jamelle Bouie and Rebecca Onion interview scholars and explore topics rarely covered in school curriculum, allowing listeners to walk away with an enriched understanding of the American past. From stories of some of the first Africans to settle in what became the United States to the ways that African Americans experienced emancipation, this series provides a college-level understanding accessible to learners at any level. This series should be required listening for all Americans as we look toward addressing historic and contemporary problems of race, justice, freedom, and equality that challenge us even in this very moment. – Daina Berry, TED-Ed Lesson:  The electrifying speeches of Sojourner Truth
 How I Built This podcast This is a great podcast to tune into during your spare time. Hosted by Guy Raz, it dives into the stories of how innovators and entrepreneurs built the companies that started a movement. Some of the great companies nowadays weren’t started intentionally. We can all appreciate a successful company, but it’s the innovators’, entrepreneurs’ and idealists’ individual stories that are really the most alluring. Plus, it’s a great reminder to me to really appreciate my own story, because who knows what movement could spawn from it? – Rob Cooke, TED Talk:  The cost of work stress – and how to reduce it
 How to Survive the End of the World podcast This podcast, hosted by activists Adrienne Maree Brown and Autumn Brown, goes even beyond what the title promises. In conversation with each other and with other amazing activists, they reveal aspects of a world in collapse and show how these fractures can be transformed into opportunities for renewal. – Yifat Susskind, TED Talk:  In uncertain times, think like a mother
 Lady Don’t Take No podcast This personal podcast from Alicia Garza, a cofounder of Black Lives Matter, is full of laughs and love, bringing on guests whose contributions to the culture are meaningful while also lifting our spirits. – Heather C. McGhee, TED Talk:  Racism has a cost for everyone
 Librivox audiobooks  I’m hugely grateful for the sustenance I’ve derived over the years from Librivox, a vast library of free, public-domain audiobooks read by generous volunteers. I often choose my books not by the author but by the reader, and one of my favorites is the late Nicholas Clifford (1930-2019), who seems to have been born to read Henry James aloud. I could graze all day on the wholegrain of Clifford’s genial tones, and I would never have thought that James’s infamously challenging “late style” could be so lucid, natural and human as it is in these recordings. Elizabeth Klett reading Nella Larsen is also outstanding. – Sascha Morrell, TED-Ed Lesson:  Why should you read  Moby Dick?
 Lore podcast I’m a historic preservation nerd who lives in an allegedly haunted house with a spooky black cat, and I have a couple of friends who work with old art museums and galleries or in architectural restoration. And we’ve all got great stories. Give us some wine or whiskey, and we’re all in for a night of creepy tales about what happens in the creaking old buildings we love so much. In lockdown, I miss these nights dearly. But  Lore, hosted by Aaron Mahnke, is a fitting substitute. Each episode rambles through a series of loosely connected stories about “the darker side of history,” but they’re told not in a way that’s meant to shock or gross out (as many modern horror movies do), but rather they’re meant to spin a great yarn. Campfires optional. – Caroline McCarthy,  TED Talk: How advertising is dividing us
 Sean Carroll’s Mindscape podcast This podcast is the place that I go to satisfy a craving for new, captivating ideas and mysteries in science, the arts, popular culture, history, philosophy and more. Sean Carroll interviews thinkers and doers in unexpected fields, ranging from poker psychology and ancient automatons to quantum physics and pandemics, and his thoughtful questions make each episode fulfilling with long-lasting nourishment for the intellect. You always feel smarter after listening to a  Mindscape session! – Adrienne Mayor, TED-Ed Lesson:  Did the Amazons really exist?
 The Moth podcast We all share this human experience and to truly understand it with empathy and love, you simply have to listen to a person tell their own story in their own words. Like moths to a flame, we come for the warmth of togetherness. I have learned so much just listening to this podcast, and I always want more. – Lee Thomas, TED Talk:  How I help people understand vitiligo
 Storytelling is such a powerful way to build empathy. I would go on walks, listen to  The Moth and often cry while listening to these stories because of the humanity that is expressed. I love the wide breadth of lived experiences that are shared — from small experiences to large experiences and people from all walks of life. – Sara Jones, TED Talk:  Reclaiming my voice as a transracial adoptee
 The Slowdown  podcast  The Slowdown quite literally does what its name suggests — it helps you pause and take a moment to breathe. Each episode is short (just 5 minutes!) and simple. Poet Tracy K. Smith begins with reflecting upon recent happenings in her life, then reads out a poem in her calm, meditative voice. I find it a great way to take a break, feel the range of emotions and vivid imagination that poetry evokes, and discover new writers. – Ananya Grover, TED Talk:  A campaign for period positivity
 The Writer’s Voice podcast and   Fiction Podcast  Literary podcasts are a staple source of mental nutrition when I’m rushing around or mired in mindless tasks, and two best enjoyed together are  The Writer’s Voice and the  Fiction Podcast, both from  The New Yorker magazine. The former features fiction from recent issues, read by the authors themselves, while the latter features writers choosing stories from back issues to read aloud and discuss with  The  New Yorker’s fiction editor, Deborah Treisman. I like to listen to an author’s new work in  The Writer’s Voice in conjunction with that author’s reading of another’s work in  The Fiction Podcast. For an appetizer, try  Anne Enright reading her story “Night Swim” for  The Writer’s Voice, then wash it down with  her reading and discussion of John Cheever’s “The Swimmer” for the   Fiction Podcast. – Sascha Morrell, TED-Ed Lesson:  Why should you read  Moby Dick?
 Touré Show  podcast episode where Zadie Smith was interviewed  Zadie Smith, as journalist Touré notes, is the best at making being smart seem really cool. I got hooked on Zadie when I read  her essay about the important differences between pleasure and joy. This interview with her makes me feel OK about interrogating my own thoughts, and she makes me want to read more to write more. She is brilliant and Touré is brilliant, and they talk about Jay-Z, writing, family, Nina Simone and the courage it takes to write with urgent integrity. The two of them share excitement over aesthetic risk in Touré’s Brooklyn apartment in a conversation that feels suspended in time. – Sara Sanford, TED Talk:  How to design gender bias out of your workplace
 The Vedic Worldview  podcast Thom Knoles is my guru and the pre-eminent master teacher of Vedic Meditation, who has taught over 40,000 people around the world to meditate. His podcast is filled with knowledge about how to live life without suffering while pursuing your life’s path. Whether you meditate or not, his wise words and calming voice will put your mind at ease. – Angel Chang, TED Talk:  How ancient textiles can help the future
 “   What’s Going On”  album    This timely classic album is rich with Marvin Gaye’s once-in-a-lifetime voice, sweeping sonic melodies, socially conscious lyrics, and spiritually hopeful musings. Released in 1971 (one year before I was born), it surprisingly sounds as if it was written for this very moment.
    The Epic of Gilgamesh Almost 5,000 years old, the story of the Mesopotamian king Gilgamesh and his search for the secret of eternal life is the earliest human tale that we have. For anyone who thinks that only modern people can wrestle with difficult issues, this epic is a lesson in humility. Read the story of Gilgamesh with its timeless portrayal of the central issues of humanity — justice, violence, sex, love, friendship, power, death — and you’ll never look at the ancient world the same way again. – Phillip Freeman, TED-Ed Lesson:  A day in the life of an ancient Celtic Druid
 “A Library of Babel”, a short story by Jorge Luis Borges (which can be  found here)  It’s almost impossible to understand how vast our universe truly is but Argentine fabulist Jorge Luis Borges gave it a try. He dreamed up a library the size of the universe in one of his most extraordinary stories “The Library of Babel”. While employed as an assistant librarian, Borges imagined a Library “composed of an indefinite and perhaps infinite number of hexagonal galleries, with vast air shafts between, surrounded by very low railings.” Like the universe, this fantastic Escher-like structure is both vividly described yet impossible to imagine — a vast universe-as-library, thought to contain every possible 410-page permutation of a basic book structure, enough to express some version of every work ever written, and everything that ever could be. In a finite world, a cosmic perspective isn’t a luxury; it is a necessity. Conveying the vastness of our universe to the public is the real challenge faced by astronomers and writers alike. – Enrico Ramirez-Ruiz, TED Talk:  Your body was forged in the spectacular death of stars
 Parable of the Sower by Octavia Butler Octavia Butler was so ahead of her time.  Recommended to me by my good friend Dr. Shamell Bell, the world Octavia creates for the reader feels eerily like some serious foreshadowing of the 2020 world that we’re currently faced with. – Joel Leon, TED Talk:  The beautiful, hard work of co-parenting
 The Alchemist  by Paulo Coelho This is one of my absolute favorite fables to read at any time because of the relevance it has no matter where you are in life. It’s timeless. It is a book, to me, that has the power to leave anyone inspired and to those who aren’t, reflective and hopefully that will lead to inspiration. It continuously helps me reshape my perception of the tests and trials of life as necessary lessons that lead to self-discovery. A fantastic book on resiliency, reflection and growth. – Rob Cooke, TED Talk:  The cost of work stress – and how to reduce it
 The Nightingale by Kristin Hannah This novel provided me with a sense of perspective during the toughest days of lockdown this year, reminding me that, however bad we think it is, there are always those who have endured more. – Lucy Hone, TED Talk:  3 secrets of resilient people
 An American Sunrise: Poems by Joy Harjo This beautiful collection of poetry paints a picture of who we are as Americans — but also who we could be — from the perspective of the first Indigenous US poet laureate. – Yifat Susskind, TED Talk:  In uncertain times, think like a mother
 The Summer Book  by Tove Jansson Especially because I live in the city, I love finding books that transport me back to nature. I just began this one, and I’m loving it. It tells the story of Sophia, a six-year-old girl awakening to existence, and Sophia’s grandmother, nearing the end of hers, as they spend the summer on a tiny, unspoiled island in the gulf of Finland. – Shantell Martin, TED Talk:  How drawing can set you free
 The Historian by Elizabeth Kostova This novel is a truly great and terrifying telling of the life of Vlad Tepes, aka Count Dracula. Its plot is innovative and riveting. Combining history, science fiction and adventure,  The Historian is not for the faint of heart, but it’s many steps above the gory nonsense of many modern-day horror stories. What if evil was as clear and simple as this story? Would life be easier? – Georges C. Benjamin, TED Conversation:  The secret weapon against pandemics
 Way of the Peaceful Warrior: A Book that Changes Lives by Dan Millman This book has been with me for many, many years, and it’s the first book I read that really forced me to take note of how the mind can be your greatest ally or foe. It also showed me to avoid too much attachment to material things and that achievements, that accomplishments alone won’t lead to a sense of purpose and fulfillment in life, and to take inventory of the simple things that I may be taking for granted. Ultimately, those tend to bring the most value. Given how the world has evolved so drastically nowadays, I’d wager it’s a book everyone can benefit from right now. – Rob Cooke, TED Talk:  The cost of work stress – and how to reduce it
 The Overstory by Richard Powers Richard Powers won the Pulitzer Prize for this massive novel about a group of initially unwitting activists fighting to save ancient redwoods in the Pacific Northwest. I teach environmental literature, and much of what I read and teach can be very depressing and apocalyptic.  The Overstory, by contrast, is oddly hopeful, a novel about environmental destruction that nonetheless leaves the reader feeling some sense of comfort. – Laura Wright, TED-Ed Lesson:  Why should you read  The God of Small Things by Arundhati Roy?
 the Lithia Trilogy (  Out of Breath,   The Ghost Runner,   The Last Mile) by Blair Richmond  I recommend this YA series about a 19-year-old long distance runner named Kat Jones and vampires. Oh, and they’re vegan vampires. – Laura Wright, TED-Ed Lesson:  Why should you read  The God of Small Things by Arundhati Roy?
 The Catcher in the Rye by J.D. Salinger  I first read this novel in my late teens, and it manages to capture feelings of isolation, loneliness and a general sense of not fitting in. At the time, Holden seemed an unwitting, but appropriate, comrade who helped establish that periods of internal turmoil are actual, lived human experiences that we all have to go through and work on. I still think about the guy sometimes, along with other Holdens of the world, and wonder how they are holding up. – France Villarta, TED Talk:  The gender-fluid history of the Philippines
 Ring of Stars by Richard Sanford Full disclosure: I am related to the author, but I’d find this novel nourishing even if it wasn’t written by family.  Ring of Stars is a story that we need. Written in 2012 as a futuristic novel, it now feels uncomfortably real. As violence, random and routine, fractures the US, the main character receives a vision. Outside of American towns that are burning like embers, he builds a safe haven: a classic drive-in theater, a circular wall bejeweled with mica and glass and shards of mirror. Standing up to the forces of our time, Sanford lights a way through the nation’s chaos, offering a glimpse of what many of us crave: shared space; safety in one another; fugitive hearts finding a place to rest. – Sara Sanford, TED Talk:  How to design gender bias out of your workplace
 Lord of the Rings by J.R.R. Tolkien I find myself re-reading  Lord of the Rings when there is upheaval or change in my life. My parents read it to me as a child, and some combination of that and Tolkien’s gorgeous writing and fantasy land, good-versus-evil vibes help center me. – Eric Sannerud, TED Talk:  Without farmers, you’d be hungry, naked and sober
 A Gentleman in Moscow by Amor Towles  I was supposed to travel to Russia — it was going to be my first visit — but then the pandemic struck. So, I made do with this novel which took me to the Metropol hotel (and no, I wasn’t actually going to stay there). And while it was an excellent portrayal of the inside of the hotel, it was an even more excellent exploration of a host of social issues, including social isolation (an unfortunate reality for many pre-pandemic and for even more during the pandemic) and issues of class. – Vinay Shandal, TED Talk:  How conscious investors can turn up the heat and make companies change
 The Wizard of the Crow by Ngugi wa Thiong’o Kenyan writer Ngugi wa Thiong’o’s political comic satire is about a fictional African nation, its megalomaniacal ruler and the toadies that cater to his every idiotic whim. Translated from Kikuyu, which is an oral and highly performative language, the narrative is expansive, magical and sprawling — more than 700 pages total. I haven’t taught it in about five years, but I keep thinking about it now be

一名年轻女子蜷缩着穿过一条狭窄的地下走廊，一只手拿着火炬，头顶崎岖的天花板被煤烟熏黑了。在洞穴黑暗中越来越深的石堆的指引下，她终于发现了她的战利品：火光照亮的墙壁上的一条血红色的岩脉。再过一万年，另一双眼睛才能再次看到它。
现在，这块血红色的岩石-一种被称为赭石的珍贵深红色矿物-再次被发现，这一次是由水下潜水员发现的，他们是几十个世纪以来第一批回到这些现已淹没的洞穴的人。科学家已经证实，该遗址现在是墨西哥昆塔纳鲁奥沿海洞穴系统的一部分，是西半球已知的最古老的赭石矿址之一。赭石被用于岩石艺术、身体装饰、晒黑兽皮，可能还有医药，是矿工们不遗余力地从中美洲丛林到非洲草原获取的奖品。
“对闪亮的红色东西的热爱是人类的一个相当普遍的特点。…。这就是我们购买红色运动伤疤的原因，“怀俄明大学的考古学家斯宾塞·佩尔顿(Spencer Pelton)说，他没有参与这项新工作，但正在怀俄明州挖掘另一个史前赭石宝藏。
新发现的遗址由大约7000年前被海平面上升淹没的三个洞穴组成。2017年，潜水员在萨吉塔里奥洞穴深处探索隧道时，注意到一些地方有破碎的钟乳石和石笋，奇怪地堆积着岩石，坑坑洼洼的墙壁和地板，以及煤烟般的天花板。这些路段距离洞口数百米，自然光无法穿透。
第二年，他们和麦克马斯特大学的地球考古学家爱德华·莱因哈特一起回来了。他们一起发现了另外几十个坑和战壕，他们认为这是采矿活动的证据。他们将这个地点命名为La Mina，西班牙语的意思是“矿井”。放射性碳年代测定显示，最早的沉积物留在大约12000年前，最年轻的大约在10000年前。通过在扫描电子显微镜下观察木炭的微小碎片，研究人员了解到煤烟来自当地高度树脂化的树木，这些树木本来可以完美地用作火炬木。
研究人员今天在“科学进展”杂志上报道，进一步的分析显示，这些古代矿工挖掘的是非常高质量的赭石。领导这项分析的密苏里大学的布兰迪·麦克唐纳解释说，矿工们用洞穴材料制作挖掘工具，而不是从外面带过来。“他们实际上是从天花板上折断钟乳石，把它们当作锤子和打桩机用来砸穿石灰石。”
2007年，研究人员在距离萨吉塔里奥不远的另一个洞穴中发现了一具绰号为Naia的12500岁古美洲少女的遗骸。因为她的存在，考古学家知道人们，包括年轻妇女，都进入了这些洞穴。麦克唐纳说，现在科学家们可能知道原因了。
没有证据可以解释矿藏是如何使用的：炎热潮湿的丛林环境已经腐烂了大多数考古线索。麦克唐纳说，除了装饰之外，赭石异常高的砷含量可能使其成为一种有效的驱虫剂。不管它的用途是什么，在上升的海平面淹没洞穴之前，大约2000年后，这个矿井似乎已经被遗弃了。目前还不清楚采矿作业停止的原因。
这项研究是对我们所知的人类与赭石长期特殊关系的一个值得欢迎的补充，芝加哥研究南美岩石艺术的人类学家克里斯托弗·范·戴维斯(Christopher Of Davis)说。佩尔顿说，研究结果还表明，人们愿意不厌其烦地拿到赭石。“我们只是真的被这种物质所吸引。”
note_en:
Crouching as she wound her way through a pinched underground corridor, a young woman grasped a torch in one hand, soot blackening the craggy ceiling above her. Guided by stacks of stones deeper and deeper in the darkness of the cave, she finally spied her prize: a blood-red vein of rock in the fire-lit wall. It would be 10,000 years before another pair of eyes saw it again.
 Now the blood-red rock—a treasured crimson mineral known as ochre—has been found again, this time by underwater divers who were the first people in tens of centuries to return to these now-submerged caves. Scientists have confirmed that the site, now part of a coastal cave system in Quintana Roo, Mexico, is one of the Western Hemisphere’s oldest known ochre mining sites. Ochre, which was used for rock art, body decoration, tanning animal hides, and possibly medicine, was a prize miners would go to great lengths to obtain,  from the jungles of Mesoamerica to the grasslands of Africa.
 “The love of shiny red things is a pretty universal human trait. … It’s why we buy red sportscars,” says Spencer Pelton, an archaeologist at the University of Wyoming who wasn’t involved in the new work, but is excavating another prehistoric ochre cache in Wyoming.
 The newly found site consists of three caves that were drowned by rising seas some 7000 years ago. In 2017, divers exploring tunnels deep within one cave, Sagitario, noticed sections with broken stalactites and stalagmites, curiously piled rocks, pitted walls and floors, and sooty ceilings. These sections were hundreds of meters from the cave’s mouth, where no natural light could penetrate.
 The next year, they returned with McMaster University geoarchaeologist Eduard Reinhardt. Together they identified dozens more pits and trenches, which they took as evidence of mining activity. They named the site  La Mina, Spanish for “the mine.” Radiocarbon dating revealed that the earliest deposits were left about 12,000 years ago and the youngest about 10,000 years ago. By looking at tiny fragments of the charcoal under a scanning electron microscope, the researchers learned the soot came from local, highly resinous trees that would have been perfect as torchwood.
 Further analysis revealed these ancient miners  were excavating remarkably high-quality ochre, the researchers report today in  Science Advances. The miners fashioned digging tools from cave materials rather than bringing them from outside, explains Brandi MacDonald from the University of Missouri, who led the analysis. “They’re actually breaking off stalactites from the ceiling and using them as hammerstones and pile drivers to smash through the limestone.”
 In 2007, researchers found the remains of  a 12,500-year-old Paleoamerican teenage girl nicknamed Naia in another cave not far from Sagitario. Because of her presence, archaeologists knew that people, including young women, entered these caves. Now, MacDonald says, scientists may know why.
 There’s no evidence to explain how the mine’s riches were used: The hot, humid jungle environment has decayed most archaeological clues. In addition to decoration, the ochre’s unusually high arsenic content could have made it an effective bug repellant, MacDonald says. Whatever its use, the mine appears to have been abandoned after about 2000 years, before the rising seas inundated the caves. It’s not clear why the mining operation halted.
 The study is a welcome addition to what we know about humans’ longstanding special relationship with ochre, says Christopher  Davis, a Chicago-based anthropologist who studies rock art in South America. The findings also suggest people were willing to go through a lot of trouble to get their hands on ochre, Pelton says. “We’re just really attracted to this substance.”

承认吧，我们都喜欢支持那些克服一切困难，通过辛勤工作、坚持不懈、坚定的决心和意志力而取得成功的失败者。
但是，一位名叫艾丹·史密斯(Aidan Smith)的推特用户却有不同的想法。根据他的说法，一些最富有和有权势的人是如何成为百万富翁和亿万富翁的故事被过于浪漫化和粉饰，因此，人们往往会遗漏一些非常重要的细节。
本月早些时候，乔恩·埃利希曼(Jon Erlichman)在推特上写道，杰夫·贝佐斯(Jeff Bezos)于1994年在自己的车库里创立了亚马逊，但他忘了提到一些非常重要的事情。
1994年的今天：杰夫·贝佐斯在车库里创立了亚马逊。pic.twitter.com/vtlu570KeX。
-Jon Erlichman(@JonErlichman)2020年7月5日。
艾丹·史密斯在回复乔恩的推文时指出，事实上，贝佐斯的父母向亚马逊投资了超过245万美元来帮助他摆脱困境。当然，我们毫无头绪。
可爱的宣传。在现实中，贝佐斯的父母在1995年给了他245,573美元，以阻止亚马逊倒闭，但从我们的右翼主流媒体将贫困归咎于个人失败，将财富归咎于个人美德，你永远不会知道这一点。https://t.co/vM15SKIcWs。
-艾丹·史密斯⧖(@AidanSmith2020)2020年7月6日
艾丹随后讲述了其他著名的亿万富翁是如何从他们有钱有势的家族成员那里获得帮助，以便创办各自的公司的。
这就是他对比尔·盖茨的评价。他说，比尔·盖茨是一个普通的人，却为了追求自己的梦想而从大学辍学的故事是不真实的。
事实上，他的母亲玛丽·盖茨是联合之路的总裁，她说服IBM雇佣微软来构建操作系统，他就是这样开始他的职业生涯的。
你可以在几乎每个亿万富翁的背景故事中找到这一点。比尔·盖茨的故事被讲述得好像他是一个普通的人，为了追求自己的梦想而从大学辍学，而实际上，他的母亲，联合之路的总裁玛丽·盖茨说服IBM聘请微软来构建一个OS pic.twitter.com/OOX1ELjMLb。
-艾丹·史密斯⧖(@AidanSmith2020)2020年7月6日。
他还接着谈到了伯克希尔哈撒韦公司(Berkshire Hathaway)首席执行官沃伦·巴菲特(Warren Buffett)。沃伦·巴菲特(Warren Buffet)这个有投资诀窍的人来自奥马哈郊区的一个中下层家庭，他的故事实际上并不全是真的。
根据艾丹的说法，实际上，他的父亲是一名国会议员，沃伦在他14岁的时候就已经投资了他父亲的农田。因此，到他上大学时，沃伦积累了9800美元的积蓄。
沃伦·巴菲特(Warren Buffett)的故事被讲述得好像他是一个斗志旺盛的暴发户，住在奥马哈郊区的一个中下层家庭里，有投资的诀窍。事实上，他的父亲是一名国会议员(鲍勃·塔夫脱的竞选经理！)。还有，呃.。pic.twitter.com/GmjyMCKYlU
-艾丹·史密斯⧖(@AidanSmith2020)2020年7月6日。
此外，如果Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)没有接受昂贵的学费，他可能就不会是今天的他。
如果艾登可信的话，马克是被他富有的父母送到飞利浦埃克塞特学院的。而且，他的寄宿费几乎是57000美元。天哪！你能相信吗？
即使你不是巨星出身，富人的孩子即使间接地得到了多少帮助，也是怎么强调都不为过的。马克·扎克伯格富有的父母把他送到菲利普斯·埃克塞特学院(学费：近57000美元的寄宿费)……。
-艾丹·史密斯⧖(@AidanSmith2020)2020年7月6日。
显然，扎克伯格甚至在进入大学之前就让软件开发人员大卫·纽曼给他做了计算机科学方面的私人辅导。
……甚至在他进入大学之前，就让软件开发人员大卫·纽曼给他做了计算机科学方面的私人辅导。扎克伯格，就像其他人提到的那样，本身就是一个聪明的人，但如果他出生在一个工人阶级家庭，他只是.。
-艾丹·史密斯⧖(@AidanSmith2020)2020年7月6日
艾登进一步解释了马克是如何在大学里被称为计算机神童的，如果他没有富有的父母，这是怎么可能的。
.就不会有和他一样的机会了。记住：人们之所以对扎克伯格如此感兴趣，一开始是因为他已经进入大学，名声是…。
-艾丹·史密斯⧖(@AidanSmith2020)2020年7月6日。
一位计算机神童，如果他的父母没有聘请软件开发人员来辅导他，这同样是不可能发生的。拥有富有的父母的好处是不可低估的，即使他们没有像贝佐斯那样给你140万英镑。没有公平的竞争环境。
-艾丹·史密斯⧖(@AidanSmith2020)2020年7月6日。
此外，他还提到了自称白手起家的亿万富翁凯莉·詹纳(Kylie Jenner)，但我们都知道她属于地球上最富有/最知名的家族之一。
当凯莉·詹纳被描述为“白手起家的亿万富翁”时，人们笑了起来，好像她不是来自地球上最富有/最知名的家族之一，但实际上，比起将盖茨/贝佐斯的财富描述为“白手起家”的精英制度产物，这并不更荒谬。
-艾丹·史密斯⧖(@AidanSmith2020)2020年7月6日
在阅读了这篇病毒式的帖子后，人们有了自己的一套观点。一些人同意艾丹的观点，另一些人则声称成功与个人的家庭背景无关。
生意就是生意，他拿了他父母的钱，然后把它变成了万亿美元的生意，不管他是怎么做到的，我永远不会理解人们对某人的成功感到压力。
-兰多(@landooo15)2020年7月6日。
是的，那笔投资让他的父母变得非常富有。对我来说，这听起来是一个很棒的决定，关于他们的End😁。
-大卫·穆勒(@David_MuellerMO)2020年7月7日。
一点儿没错。这都是宣传。要想富有，你要么碰巧碰上好运，要么成为罪犯，要么出生在相对富裕的家庭。
-大阪(@ocgeorge_)2020年7月7日。
他的继父是一个真实的从穷到富的故事。他小时候来自古巴，一无所有，后来自学成才成为埃克森石油公司(Exon Oil)的一名高管。他为亚马逊提供了大部分种子资金。
-Darryl Cunningham(@AcmeDarryl)2020年7月6日。
这是微不足道的……如果我拥有一家企业，我需要资金，而且我已经用尽了所有正常的融资途径……我也会问我的父母。如果他们有房屋或其他房产的流动性，245克朗作为有抵押品的贷款并不难获得。抱歉，这太蠢了。
-魔兽世界是我的社交距离(@GDGivens)2020年7月7日。
这表明你要成功所需要的就是热血、汗水和..。*Check Notes*几代人积累的资产往往建立在穷人的劳动基础上。
-Mito(@线粒体95)2020年7月6日。
所以，伙计们，这条病毒式的帖子的全部意义就是给我们上了非常重要的一课。不要因为不能实现目标而责备自己，因为即使是最成功的人也得到了经济上的帮助和支持。
note_en:
Admit it, we all love rooting for underdogs who overcome all odds and make it big through hard work, perseverance, sheer determination and force of will.
 But, Twitter user named  Aidan Smith thinks differently. According to him, stories about how some of the richest and powerful people became millionaires and billionaires are too romanticized and glossed over, as a result of which, people tend to miss out some of the very important details.
  Earlier this month, Jon Erlichman tweeted about how Jeff Bezos founded Amazon in 1994 in his own garage but, he forgot to mention something very important.
 On this day in 1994: Amazon was founded by Jeff Bezos in his garage.  pic.twitter.com/vtlu570KeX
— Jon Erlichman (@JonErlichman)  July 5, 2020
 Replying to Jon's tweet, Aidan Smith pointed out that in fact, Bezos' parents invested more than 245k dollars into Amazon to help him out. Of course, we had no clue.
 Cute propaganda. In reality Bezos's mommy and daddy gave him $245,573 to stop Amazon from failing in 1995, but you'd never know it from listening to our right-wing mainstream media that blames poverty on personal failure and attributes wealth to personal virtue.  https://t.co/vM15SKIcWs
— Aidan Smith ⧖ (@AidanSmith2020)  July 6, 2020
 Aidan then went to talk about how other famous billionaires received help from their wealthy and powerful family members in order to start their respective companies.
 This is what he had to say about Bill Gates. He states the story of Bill Gates being a normal guy who dropped out of college to pursue his dreams is not true.
 In fact, his mother Mary Gates, the president of United Way, convinced IBM to hire Microsoft to build an OS and that's how he started his career.
 You can find this in the backstory of almost every billionaire. The story of Bill Gates is told as if he was a normal guy who dropped out of college to pursue his dream when in reality his mom Mary Gates, the president of United Way, convinced IBM to hire Microsoft to build an OS  pic.twitter.com/OOX1ELjMLb
— Aidan Smith ⧖ (@AidanSmith2020)  July 6, 2020
 He also goes on to talk about Warren Buffett, CEO of Berkshire Hathaway. The tale of Warren Buffet, the guy who had a knack for investing, coming from a lower-middle class suburban home in Omaha actually isn't all that true.
 As per Aidan, in reality his dad was a congressman and Warren had already invested in his father's farmland when he was 14-years-old. So, by the time he reached college, Warren accumulated $9,800 in his savings.
 The tale of Warren Buffett is told as if he was a scrappy upstart living in a lower-middle class suburban home in Omaha who had a knack for investing. In reality his dad was a congressman (and Bob Taft’s campaign manager!) and, uh...  pic.twitter.com/GmjyMCKYlU
— Aidan Smith ⧖ (@AidanSmith2020)  July 6, 2020
 Also, Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg might not have been who he is today, had he not received expensive tuition.
 If Aidan is to be believed, Mark was sent to Philips Exeter Academy by his wealthy parents. And, his tuition fees for boarding was almost $57,000. OMG! Can you believe it?
 Even if you’re not born to mega-celebrities it really can’t be stressed enough how much a leg up children of the wealthy get even indirectly. Mark Zuckeberg’s wealthy parents sent him to Phillips Exeter Academy (tuition: almost $57,000 for boarding)...
— Aidan Smith ⧖ (@AidanSmith2020)  July 6, 2020
 Apparently, Zuckerberg also got software developer David Newman to give him private tutoring in computer science before he even entered college.
 ...and got software developer David Newman to give him private tutoring in computer science before he even entered college. Zuckerberg, is, like others mentioned, an intelligent individual in his own right, but if he was born into a working-class family he simply...
— Aidan Smith ⧖ (@AidanSmith2020)  July 6, 2020
 Aidan further explains how Mark was known as a computer prodigy in college and how that wouldn't have been possible had he not had wealthy parents.
 ...wouldn’t have had the same opportunities as he did. Remember: People took such an interest in Zuckerberg to begin with because he already entered college with the reputation as...
— Aidan Smith ⧖ (@AidanSmith2020)  July 6, 2020
 a computing prodigy, which, again, couldn’t have happened if his parents didn’t hire a software developer to tutor him. The benefits of having wealthy parents, even if they don’t give you a 1/4 million as Bezos’s did, can’t be underestimated. There is no fair playing field.
— Aidan Smith ⧖ (@AidanSmith2020)  July 6, 2020
 And, he also mentions Kylie Jenner who claims to be a self-made billionaire but, we all know she belongs to one of the wealthiest/best-known families on Earth.
 People laugh when Kylie Jenner is described as a “self-made billionaire” as if she doesn’t come from one of the wealthiest/best-known families on Earth, but really, it’s no more absurd then describing Gates’/Bezos’ wealth as being a “self-made” product of meritocracy.
— Aidan Smith ⧖ (@AidanSmith2020)  July 6, 2020
 After reading this viral thread, people had their own set of opinions. While some agreed with Aidan, others claimed that success had nothing to do with an individual's family background.
 business is business, he took his parents money and flipped it to a trillion dollar business, nevermind how he got there, i will never understand people being pressed about someone succeeding
— lando (@Landoooo15)  July 6, 2020
   Yes and that investment made his parents extremely wealthy. To me, that sounds like a great decision on their end 😁
— David Mueller (@David_MuellerMO)  July 7, 2020
 Exactly. It’s all propaganda.  To be wealthy, you either have to stumble on good luck, be a criminal, or be born into relative wealth
— Osaji (@ocgeorge_)  July 7, 2020
 His step dad is the real rags-to-riches story. He came from Cuba as a boy with nothing and educated himself up to be a Exon Oil exec. He supplied much of the seed money for Amazon.
— Darryl Cunningham (@AcmeDarryl)  July 6, 2020
  This is petty...If I owned a business and I needed capital and I had exhausted all the normal capital paths...I'd ask my parents too. If they have the liquidity in a home or other property, 245k would not have been hard to get as a loan with collateral. Sorry this is dumb.
— World of Warcraft is My Social Distancing (@GDGivens)  July 7, 2020
 Goes to show that all you need to succeed is blood, sweat, and... *checks notes* multiple generations of amassed assets often built on the labor of the poor.
— Mito (@Mitochondria95)  July 6, 2020
 So, guys the whole point of this viral thread is to teach us a very important lesson. Don't beat yourself up for not being able to achieve your goals because even the most successful person received financial help and support.
title: 这条病毒式的帖子正在打破关于著名亿万富翁的神话，他们显然是从“贫穷”开始的
title_en: This Viral Thread Is Busting Myths About Famous Billionaires Who Apparently Started Out 'Poor'

note:
周日晚间，高通风险投资公司(Qualcomm Ventures)表示，将向Reliance Jio平台投资9700万美元，收购这家印度顶级电信运营商0.15%的股权。
高通首席执行官史蒂夫·莫伦科普夫(Steve Mollenkopf)表示，该公司相信，通过提供降价语音和数据计划扰乱了印度电信市场的Reliance Jio平台，未来将“为印度消费者提供一套新的服务和体验”。
在印度与Bharti Airtel和沃达丰Idea竞争的Reliance Jio平台，在成立不到四年的时间里，已经积累了近4亿订户，并成为世界第二大互联网市场的顶级运营商。
它在印度电信运营商中的主导地位，同时保持了与竞争对手相当的ARPU(每用户平均收入)，这使得印度顶级公司信实工业(Reliance Industries)的子公司Reliance Jio Platforms成为一家对一批知名投资者具有吸引力的公司。Facebook、银湖(Silver Lake)、通用大西洋(General Atlantic)、英特尔(Intel)等公司在全球疫情最严重的时候支持Jio平台。
“凭借无与伦比的速度和不断涌现的使用案例，5G有望在未来几年改变每个行业。Jio Platforms通过其广泛的数字和技术能力引领了印度的数字革命。作为在印度长期存在的推动者和投资者，我们期待着在Jio的愿景中发挥作用，进一步变革印度的数字经济，“Mollenkopf在一份声明中表示。
近几个月来，一些投资者告诉TechCrunch，Reliance Jio Platforms的所有者以及他与印度执政党的密切关系，也是信实工业(Reliance Industries)数字部门对许多人如此有吸引力的关键。他们认为，购买Jio平台的股份将减轻他们的监管负担，因为他们在印度做了更多的业务。
支持Reliance Jio平台的12家公司中的一家的一位知情人士表示，这家印度公司也很有吸引力，因为全球公司都在努力减少对中国的敞口和依赖。
note_en:
On Sunday evening,  Qualcomm Ventures   said it will invest $97 million in  Reliance Jio   Platforms to acquire a 0.15% equity stake in the top Indian telecom operator.
 Steve Mollenkopf, chief executive of Qualcomm, said the firm believes that Reliance Jio Platforms, which has disrupted the Indian telecommunications market by offering cut-rate voice and data plans, “will deliver a new set of services and experiences to Indian consumers” in the future.
  Reliance Jio Platforms, which competes with Bharti Airtel and Vodafone Idea in India, has amassed nearly 400 million subscribers and has become the top carrier in the world’s second largest internet market in less than four years of its existence.
 Its dominance in the Indian telecom operator while maintaining an ARPU (average revenue per user) that match those of its rivals has made Reliance Jio Platforms — a subsidiary of India’s top firm Reliance Industries — an attractive firm for a roster of high-profile investors. Facebook, Silver Lake, General Atlantic, Intel are some of the firms that have backed Jio Platforms at the height of a global pandemic.
 “With unmatched speeds and emerging use cases, 5G is expected to transform every industry in the coming years. Jio Platforms has led the digital revolution in India through its extensive digital and technological capabilities. As an enabler and investor with a longstanding presence in India, we look forward to playing a role in Jio’s vision to further revolutionize India’s digital economy,” said Mollenkopf in a statement.
  Some investors have told TechCrunch in recent months that Reliance Jio Platforms’ owner and his closeness with the ruling political party in India is also crucial to why the digital unit of Reliance Industries is so attractive to many. They believe that buying a stake in Jio Platforms would lower the regulatory burden on them as they do more businesses in India.
 A person familiar with the matter at one of the 12 firms that has backed Reliance Jio Platforms said that the Indian firm is also enticing as globally companies are trying to cut down on their Chinese exposure and reliance.
title: 高通风险投资称将向Reliance Jio平台投资9700万美元收购0.15%股权
title_en: Qualcomm Ventures says it will invest $97M in Reliance Jio Platforms to acquire a 0.15% equity stake

note:
在两个超级大国之间的紧张局势加剧之际，美国国务院对在华美国公民发出了正式警告。它说，美国人可能面临无解释的驱逐或拘留。
美国国务院警告在华美国公民，中国当局任意拘留的风险增加。
国务院特别告诫不要因为发送对政府至关重要的私人电子信息而受到驱逐或拘留的威胁。
周六发布的这份咨询报告称，美国公民可能会被拘留，但无法获得美国领事服务或有关他们涉嫌犯罪的信息。
国务院将这些规定描述为出于维护法律和秩序以外的目的任意执行当地法律。
美国和中国的关系最近在冠状病毒、贸易和香港问题上跌至谷底。
美国总统唐纳德·特朗普周五告诉记者，与中国的关系已经严重受损。
美国一直直言不讳地批评6月份强加给香港的有争议的安全法。这项立法禁止在世界任何地方对中国政府进行颠覆行为和言论。
特朗普还多次指责中国对冠状病毒危机的处理，以及他所说的在大流行早期未能沟通准确信息。
北京也因报道中国西部西藏和新疆侵犯人权的报道而受到抨击。
本周，华盛顿宣布对三名官员实施签证禁令和资产冻结，其中包括新疆市委书记陈全国，他是北京方面针对动荡不安的少数民族强硬政策的设计师。
由于担心被任意拘留，澳大利亚和加拿大等其他国家也发布了旅行警告。中国被指控任意拘留与官员意见不同的外国公民。
note_en:
Amid increased tensions between the two superpowers, the US State Department has issued a formal warning for US citizens in China. It said Americans may face deportation or detention without explanation.
    The US State Department has warned US citizens in China of an increased risk of arbitrary detention by authorities.
  The State Department specifically cautioned against the threat of deportation or detainment "for sending private electronic messages critical" of the government.
  "US citizens may be detained without access to US consular services or information about their alleged crime," the advisory, published on Saturday, said.
    The State Department described the rules as "arbitrary enforcement of local laws for purposes other than maintaining law and order.
    Relations between the US and China have hit rock bottom recently over the coronavirus, trade and Hong Kong.
  US President Donald Trump told reporters Friday that the relationship with China had been "severely damaged."
      The US has been vocally critical of the controversial security law pushed on Hong Kong in June. The legislation bans subversive acts and speech against the Chinese government made anywhere in the world.
  Trump has also repeatedly blamed China for their handling of the coronavirus crisis and what he describes as their failure to communicate accurate information early in the pandemic.
  Beijing has also come under fire for reports of human rights abuses in Tibet and Xinjiang provinces in the west of the country.
    This week, Washington announced visa bans and an assets freeze on three officials, including Chen Quanguo, the Communist Party chief in Xinjiang and architect of Beijing's hardline policies against restive minorities.
  Australia and Canada are among the other countries that also issued travel warnings over fears of arbitrary detentions. China has been accused of arbitrarily detaining foreign citizens who disagree with officials.
title: 美国公民警告称，他们在中国面临任意逮捕
title_en: US citizens warned they face arbitrary arrest in China

note:
科学家们已经设计出一种方法，可以利用新冠肺炎幸存者富含抗体的血浆进行上臂注射，他们说这种方法可以让人们接种几个月的病毒疫苗。
医生和其他专家表示，使用已被证明在预防甲型肝炎等其他疾病方面有效的技术，这些注射将被用于高危医护人员、疗养院患者，甚至在公共免下车地点-潜在地保护数百万人的生命。
率先提出这一建议的两名科学家-一名83岁的带状疱疹研究人员和一名艾滋病基因治疗专家-已经获得了领先的血液和免疫学专家的广泛支持，包括那些在国家新冠肺炎血浆研究中心的科学家。
但这个想法只存在于纸面上。《洛杉矶时报》的一项调查显示，联邦官员已经两次拒绝了讨论这项提议的请求，制药公司--甚至承认该计划可能会有效果--也拒绝设计或制造这些针剂。对开发免疫疫苗缺乏兴趣的同时，联邦政府对迟缓的大流行反应进行了更严格的审查。
这个想法很有希望，这一点几乎没有分歧；争议的是时机问题。联邦卫生官员和行业组织表示，基于血浆的疗法的开发应该专注于治疗已经患病的人，而不是预防那些仍然健康的人受到感染。
美国国立卫生研究院国家过敏和传染病研究所所长安东尼·福奇博士说，像疫苗一样起作用的上臂注射是一个非常有吸引力的概念。
然而，他说，科学家应该首先证明目前在全国医院病房静脉注射给患者的冠状病毒抗体确实有效。一旦你展示了它的功效，那么很明显下一步就是把它转换成肌肉注射。
但质疑延迟的科学家认为，免疫注射很容易扩大规模，应该立即进入临床试验。他们说，在有疫苗之前，注射疫苗是在流感大流行的关键时刻预防数百万潜在感染的唯一看似合理的方法。
梅奥诊所研究员迈克尔·乔伊纳(Michael Joyner)博士说，除了错失了一个机会，这真的是一个令人费解的事情。乔伊纳博士领导着一个由美国食品和药物管理局(FDA)赞助的项目，利用新冠肺炎幸存者的冠状病毒抗体。这似乎很明显。
所谓的恢复期血浆的使用已经变得很普遍。超过28,000名患者已经接受了静脉注射治疗，初步数据表明这种方法是安全的。研究人员还在研究静脉滴注产品是否能防止新的感染扎根。
血浆中的抗体可以被浓缩，并通过一种名为免疫球蛋白(Ig)的药物传递给患者，这种药物可以通过静脉滴注或注射给药。几十年来，免疫球蛋白疫苗一直用于预防一系列疾病；预防甲型肝炎的免疫球蛋白疫苗于1944年首次获得许可。它们可用于治疗最近接触过乙型肝炎、破伤风、水痘和狂犬病的患者。
乔伊纳告诉泰晤士报，每天有600名新冠肺炎幸存者捐献他们的血浆，根据捐献量和浓度的不同，可以产生多达5,000张Ig疫苗。他说，美国有数百万可能的幸存者，容量不是问题。
血浆公司表示，他们已经将精力集中在对病情最严重的患者的干预上。例如，格里夫斯表示，它之所以没有开发疫苗，是因为它正在寻求联邦政府支持的静脉注射配方，以治疗已经感染严重新冠肺炎病例的患者。但该公司承认，抗体注射将是一个很好的预防选择。
免疫注射的倡导者表示，企业不愿投资于可能很快被疫苗取代的产品，因此政府应该提供财政激励措施来抵消这一风险。他们说，联邦政府已经通过“翘曲速度行动”(Operation Warp Speed)在疫苗研究上花费了数十亿美元，而为免疫球蛋白注射提供资金，可能会成为通往疫苗的桥梁，价格相对较低。
抗体是目前地球上最宝贵的资源，仅次于空气。美国Ig产品的主要分销商FFF Enterprise的首席执行官帕特里克·施密特(Patrick Schmidt)表示：我们拥有生产经过验证的产品的行业、技术和诀窍。
投入到一种疫苗上的资金和资源的数量，但不能保证它会起作用-这到现在已经可以拯救生命了。
关于注射方法预防冠状病毒的建议来自一位免疫研究人员，他从历史中获得了灵感。
迈克尔·奥克斯曼博士知道，即使在1918年流感大流行期间，康复患者的血液似乎也有助于治疗其他人。从那时起，恢复期血浆被用于抗击麻疹和严重急性呼吸综合征(SARS)等疾病。
像其他医生一样，奥克斯曼猜测，在有限的时间内，流经冠状病毒幸存者静脉的血液可能含有免疫丰富的抗体，这些抗体可以预防或帮助治疗感染。
3月27日，他和加州希望之城基因治疗中心主任约翰·扎亚(John Zaia)博士向联邦生物医学高级研究与发展局(BARDA)提交了一份提案，敦促为急救人员和其他高危群体成员推出免疫球蛋白注射。
该机构向Grifols提供了1,250万美元，向Emergent BioSolutions提供了1,450万美元，用于在抗击流感的50多个不同的生物医学合作伙伴关系中，生产基于血浆的IV形式的新冠肺炎药物。但是豁免注射的提议被拒绝了。
两人随后提出了在加州大学圣地亚哥分校进行临床试验的详细提案。他们认为，受过最低培训的医护人员可以迅速注射5毫升的Ig，为医生和护士以及疗养院、大学宿舍和军用潜艇的居民提供至少两个月的免疫力。
记录显示，这份意见书得到了其他四名传染病研究人员和统计学家的支持，但也被拒绝了。
BARDA的一位女发言人告诉“泰晤士报”，该机构已经收到了数千份意见书，虽然我们对免疫球蛋白(Ig)在治疗和预防方面的潜力很感兴趣，但我们专注于住院患者的治疗，以拯救生命。
这一策略让奥克斯曼和扎亚感到困惑，他们说Ig注射是一种更高效的传递系统，有可能接触到更多的人。
乔伊纳说，更重要的是，预防性注射可能比静脉注射需要的抗体要少得多。他说，有了搞笑疫苗，血浆捐赠可能会达到两倍甚至五倍。
如果第二波疫苗在有效疫苗之前到来，那么储备将变得更加重要。
奥克斯曼开始将注意力集中在该行业的关键参与者-主导血浆药物开发的制造商身上。他每周与经销商施密特通电话；两人一起试图说服七家不同的公司自己生产疫苗，并将其带到医疗机构进行检测。他们没有成功。
武田和CSL Behring这两家大公司共同领导了新的CoVIg-19血浆联盟，以开发一种用于静脉滴注的Ig产品，他们表示，他们的努力针对的是病情最严重的人。武田公司血浆衍生疗法业务部负责人朱莉·金(Julie Kim)表示，假设试验成功，静脉注射配方代表着接触患者的最快途径。
财务计算可能是公司的另一个因素。供应专家说，静脉血浆产品传统上是该行业的主要经济驱动力，部分原因是疫苗多年来已经取代了许多短期免疫注射。赚钱的抗体在静脉注射药物中的稀释程度也远远高于注射药物，这提高了利润率。
他们从医院的静脉注射药物中收取了一大笔钱。一位传染病专家表示，他们不想把制造工厂投入到不会赚很多钱的事情上。这位专家主张注射冠状病毒Ig，但要求不公开姓名。
研究人员还表示，考虑到一种更持久的疫苗可能在一年内取代冠状病毒，行业高管几乎没有动机为冠状病毒生产免疫疫苗。
Grifols的一位发言人告诉时报，选择一种或另一种交付方式与潜在的财务或定价影响无关。
整个5月，耶鲁大学、哈佛大学、约翰·霍普金斯大学、杜克大学和加州大学四所不同学校的研究人员和医生向数十名立法者发出了接二连三的信件。他们在国会山与卫生政策主管举行了虚拟会议，但表示到目前为止还没有听到任何后续行动。
国家新冠肺炎恢复期血浆项目主席Arturo Casadevall博士说，他与食品和药物管理局官员交谈，这些官员告诉他，他们不会指示公司生产什么。卡萨德瓦尔告诉“泰晤士报”，国家项目的领导人非常支持快速开发免疫球蛋白的必要性，他相信这将对遏制疫情非常有帮助。
梅奥诊所的乔伊纳说，美国可能有1000万到2000万人携带冠状病毒抗体-而且这个数字还在不断攀升。如果他们中只有2%的人在三个不同的场合捐献标准的800毫升血浆，仅他们的血浆就可以为高危美国人产生数百万剂免疫球蛋白。
乔伊纳说，无论是在热点肉类加工厂，还是在商场外停车场的移动设备，相信我，你都能买到血浆。这既不是生物问题，也不是技术问题。这是一个粗略的情报问题。
目前，抗体注射似乎不是政府或行业的高度优先事项。
4月28日，也就是美国确诊的冠状病毒病例超过100万例的同一天，Grifols发布了一项重大产品声明，将扩大其在免疫球蛋白治疗疾病方面的领导地位。
note_en:
Scientists have devised a way to use the antibody-rich blood plasma of Covid-19 survivors for an upper-arm injection that they say could inoculate people against the virus for months.
 Using technology that's been proven effective in preventing other diseases such as hepatitis A, the injections would be administered to high-risk health care workers, nursing home patients, or even at public drive-through sites — potentially protecting millions of lives, the doctors and other experts say.
    The two scientists who spearheaded the proposal — an 83-year-old shingles researcher and his counterpart, an HIV gene therapy expert — have garnered widespread support from leading blood and immunology specialists, including those at the center of the nation's Covid-19 plasma research.
 But the idea exists only on paper. Federal officials have twice rejected requests to discuss the proposal, and pharmaceutical companies — even acknowledging the likely efficacy of the plan — have declined to design or manufacture the shots, according to a Los Angeles Times investigation. The lack of interest in launching development of immunity shots comes amid heightened scrutiny of the federal government's sluggish pandemic response.
 There is little disagreement that the idea holds promise; the dispute is over the timing. Federal health officials and industry groups say the development of plasma-based therapies should focus on treating people who are already sick, not on preventing infections in those who are still healthy.
 Dr. Anthony Fauci, director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases at the NIH, said an upper-arm injection that would function like a vaccine "is a very attractive concept."
 However, he said, scientists should first demonstrate that the coronavirus antibodies that are currently delivered to patients intravenously in hospital wards across the country actually work. "Once you show the efficacy, then the obvious next step is to convert it into an intramuscular" shot.
 But scientists who question the delay argue that the immunity shots are easy to scale up and should enter clinical trials immediately. They say that until there's a vaccine, the shots offer the only plausible method for preventing potentially millions of infections at a critical moment in the pandemic.
 "Beyond being a lost opportunity, this is a real head-scratcher," said Dr. Michael Joyner, a Mayo Clinic researcher who leads a program sponsored by the Food and Drug Administration to capitalize on coronavirus antibodies from COVID-19 survivors. "It seems obvious."
 The use of so-called convalescent plasma has already become widespread. More than 28,000 patients have already received the IV treatment, and preliminary data suggest that the method is safe. Researchers are also looking at whether the IV drip products would prevent new infections from taking root.
 The antibodies in plasma can be concentrated and delivered to patients through a type of drug called immune globulin, or Ig, which can be given through either an IV drip or a shot. Ig shots have for decades been used to prevent an array of diseases; the Ig shot that prevents hepatitis A was first licensed in 1944. They are available to treat patients who have recently been exposed to hepatitis B, tetanus, varicella and rabies.
  Joyner told the Times that 600 COVID-19 survivors donating their plasma each day could, depending on donation volumes and concentrations, generate up to 5,000 Ig shots. With millions of probable survivors in the United States, he said, capacity isn't a problem.
 Plasma companies said they've focused their efforts on an intervention for the sickest patients. Grifols, for example, said it has not developed a shot because it is pursuing a federally supported IV formula "to treat patients already infected with a serious case of COVID-19," but the company acknowledged that an antibody injection would be a good choice for prevention.
 Advocates for the immunity shots say businesses are reluctant to invest in a product that could soon be replaced by a vaccine, so the government should offer financial incentives to offset that risk. Billions of federal dollars are already being spent on vaccine research through Operation Warp Speed, and funding for an Ig shot that could serve as a bridge to a vaccine would come with a relatively modest price tag, they say.
 "Antibodies are the most precious resource on the planet right now, next to air. We have the industry, the technology and the know-how to produce a proven product," said Patrick Schmidt, the chief executive of FFF Enterprises, a major distributor of Ig products in the United States.
 "The amount of money and resources going into a vaccine, with no guarantee it will work — this could have saved lives by now."
 The proposal for an injection approach to coronavirus prevention came from an immunization researcher who drew his inspiration from history.
 Dr. Michael Oxman knew that, even during the 1918 flu pandemic, the blood of recovered patients appeared to help treat others. Since then, convalescent plasma has been used to fight measles and severe acute respiratory syndrome, or SARS, among other diseases.
 Like other doctors, Oxman surmised that, for a limited time, the blood coursing through the veins of coronavirus survivors probably contains immune-rich antibodies that could prevent — or help treat — an infection.
 On March 27, he and Dr. John Zaia, the director of City of Hope's Center for Gene Therapy in California, submitted a proposal to the federal Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority, or BARDA, urging the rollout of Ig shots for first responders and members of other high-risk groups.
 The agency granted $12.5 million to Grifols and $14.5 million to Emergent BioSolutions to produce plasma-based COVID-19 medicines in IV form drips, among more than 50 different biomedical partnerships to fight the pandemic. But the immunity shot proposal was rejected.
    The pair followed up with a detailed proposal to conduct a clinical trial at the University of California, San Diego. They believed injectable 5-milliliter vials of Ig could be given quickly by minimally trained health care workers, offering at least two months of immunity to doctors and nurses, as well as residents of nursing homes, college dormitories and military submarines.
 The submission was backed by four other infectious disease researchers and statisticians, but it was also rejected, records show.
 A spokeswoman for BARDA told the Times that the agency had received thousands of submissions, and that "while we are interested in the potential of (Ig) for treatment and prevention, we are focused intently on treatments for hospitalized patients to save lives."
 The strategy baffled Oxman and Zaia, who said the Ig shots are a far more efficient delivery system that can potentially reach many more people.
 What's more, prophylactic shots would probably require far fewer antibodies than IV treatments, Joyner said. With Ig shots, plasma donations could possibly go twice — or even five times — as far, he said.
 If a second wave were to arrive before an effective vaccine, that stockpile would be all the more essential.
 Oxman started focusing his attention on the key players in the industry — the manufacturers who dominate the development of plasma drugs. He held weekly phone calls with Schmidt, the distributor; together, the two tried to convince seven different companies to produce the shots themselves and bring them to health agencies for testing. They were unsuccessful.
 Takeda and CSL Behring, two large companies who co-lead the new CoVIg-19 Plasma Alliance to develop an Ig product for IV drips, said their efforts are trained on the sickest. The IV formula "represents the fastest path to reach patients, assuming the trial is successful," said Julie Kim, the head of the plasma-derived therapies business unit at Takeda.
 Financial calculations may be another factor for companies. Intravenous plasma products are traditionally the main economic driver for the industry, supply experts said, in part because vaccines have replaced many short-term immunity shots over the years. The money-making antibodies are also far more diluted in intravenous drugs than in injectable ones, which boosts profit margins.
 "They charge a fortune off of intravenous drugs in the hospital. They don't want to devote the manufacturing plant to something that won't make oodles of money," said one infectious disease expert, who has advocated for coronavirus Ig shots but asked not to be publicly identified.
 Researchers also said industry executives have little incentive to produce the immunity shots for the coronavirus, given the possibility that a longer-lasting vaccine could replace it within a year.
  "The choice of one delivery method or another has no connection with the potential financial or pricing implications," a Grifols spokesman told the Times.
 Throughout May, researchers and doctors at Yale, Harvard, Johns Hopkins, Duke and four different University of California schools sent a barrage of letters to dozens of lawmakers. They held virtual meetings with health policy directors on Capitol Hill, but say they have heard no follow-up to date.
 Dr. Arturo Casadevall, the chair of the National COVID-19 Convalescent Plasma Project, said he spoke to FDA officials who told him they do not instruct companies on what to produce. Casadevall told the Times that the leaders of the national project were "very supportive of the need to develop" an Ig shot rapidly and that he believed it would be "very helpful in stemming the epidemic."
 Joyner, of the Mayo Clinic, said there are probably 10 million to 20 million people in the U.S. carrying coronavirus antibodies — and the number keeps climbing. If just 2% of them were to donate a standard 800 ml of plasma on three separate occasions, their plasma alone could generate millions of Ig shots for high-risk Americans.
 "At a hot-spot meatpacking plant, or at a mobile unit in the parking lot outside a mall — trust me, you can get the plasma," Joyner said. "This is not a biological problem nor a technology problem. It's a back-of-the-envelope intelligence problem."
 The antibody injections, for now, do not appear to be a high priority for the government or the industry.
 Grifols, on April 28 — the same day that the U.S. topped 1 million confirmed coronavirus cases — made a major product announcement that would "expand its leadership in disease treatment with immunoglobulins."

De Devsu的联合创始人兼首席执行官。我们为设计和制造数字产品的远程团队提供访问权限。
毫无疑问，由于新冠肺炎的缘故，异地办公排在了加速趋势排行榜的首位。随着公司实施这些新政策，思考远程工作的原因和方式至关重要。
在我的公司，远程工作从一开始就是一个特点。然而，从历史上看，我们的方法是分布式的团队，而不是分布式的个人。这意味着我们的团队大部分时间都在同一个屋檐下，而我们与客户进行远程工作。分散的个人意味着每个团队成员将在与团队成员和客户一起工作的同时，从他们所在的位置远程工作。对我们来说，向分布式个人团队的过渡是有机的。然而，领导者需要了解远程团队的好处和挑战，以及它如何融入公司文化和战略。
让我们来看看从家里过渡到工作的一些好处。
制造者的创造性时间表：你的公司有可能雇佣了大量的知识型员工。远程设置可帮助工程师、作家、设计师等不间断地延长工作时间。更少的干扰和即兴会议，人们可以围绕计划好的会议组织得更好。
灵活性：说到在家工作，很多人不一定喜欢。他们喜欢的是它提供的灵活性，比如不必通勤，有更多的时间处理家庭和个人事务。
人才：通过远程设置，公司可以进入新市场寻找人才并保持竞争力。地理套利将不再是一种竞争优势，而是一种普遍做法。
降低成本：组织将能够节省办公空间和相关费用。另一方面，员工将能够生活在生活成本较低的城市。
工作效率：在家工作时几乎不会损失工作效率。最近的一项研究显示，当人们在家工作时，只有1%的生产力损失。然而，每家公司都是不同的，所以监控生产率以确保不会产生负面影响是一个好主意。远程工作还有助于更快地揭露表现不佳的员工，当这种类型的问题出现时，管理层将面临更大的压力来解决它。
尽管在家工作的政策有很多好处，但领导层也需要意识到几个挑战，以应对和减轻它们的影响。
沟通和会议质量：经理工作的一个重要方面就是沟通。不管我们喜欢与否，会议都是我们日常互动和工作的重要组成部分。有充分的记录表明，面对面的会议比在线会议更有效。在线交流对人们的认知负荷有影响。创意团队也是如此；同一屋檐下的协作比在线协作更有效。
沟通流程：沟通是知识工作和服务业务的关键方面。远程工作往往会增加异步通信。对于许多公司来说，沟通流程需要同步，并且需要快速发生，以避免内部和外部团队的瓶颈和等待时间。远程团队沟通往往更加机械化，非正式对话发生的频率也较低。
职业发展：许多远程工作的人报告说，他们的职业和职业发展面临更多挑战。很多职业发展机会都发生在办公室的走廊里、即兴的社交聚会和会议上。对于远程工作者来说，这些社交场合几乎是不存在的。距离也会减少队友之间的良性竞争。
工作效率和工作与个人生活之间的界限：在家工作的员工有更多与工作无关的事情(孩子、宠物、家人等)。很多时候，他们发现很难平衡工作和个人生活。此外，他们可能没有正确的设置来在家工作，这可能会影响他们的工作效率。
不平等加剧：在家工作可能会加剧不平等，因为生活条件较好的人在家里有更好的条件，例如私人办公室。这将使他们在提高效率和生产力方面占据上风。
组织学习：组织学习有正式的和非正式的两种方式。远程工作人员往往很少有机会无意中听到有趣的转换，在走廊里进行讨论，或者只是即兴发表一些评论，引发一场深思熟虑的讨论。
扩展：分散的员工很难扩展。据报道，大公司很难过渡到偏远的工作场所。
公司应该着眼于放大迁移到分散的劳动力带来的好处，并将挑战降至最低。从长远来看，大流行不应该是公司实施这些类型政策的主要原因。组织应该选择这种方法，因为他们相信这将使他们更有竞争力、更有效率、更好。

note_en:
Co-founder & CEO of   Devsu . We provide access to remote teams that design and build digital products.
    There is no doubt remote work is on the top of the list of accelerating trends as a result of Covid-19. As companies put in place these new policies, it is crucial to think about the "why" and the "how" of remote working.
  At my company, remote work has been a feature since day one. However, historically our approach has been of a distributed team, not of distributed individuals. What this means is that our team has been under the same roof, most of the time, while we work remotely with our clients. Distributed individuals mean that each team member will be working remotely, from their location, while working with teammates and clients. For us, the transition to a team of distributed individuals has been organic. However, leaders need to understand the benefits as well as the challenges of a remote team and how it fits into company culture and strategy.
    Let's take a look at some of the benefits of transitioning to working from home.
  Creative schedule for makers: The chances are that your company employs a lot of knowledge workers. A remote setup helps engineers, writers, designers, etc. to have more extended periods of work time without interruption. There are fewer distractions and impromptu meetings, and people can organize better around planned meetings.
  Flexibility: When it comes to working from home, many people do not necessarily like it. What they like is the flexibility it provides, such as not having to commute and more time for their families and personal matters.
    Talent: A remote setup allows companies to tap into new markets to find talent and stay competitive. Geographic arbitrage will not be a competitive advantage anymore, but a common practice.
  Cost reductions: Organizations will be able to save in office space and related expenses. On the other hand, employees will be able to live in cities with lower costs of living.
  Productivity: There is little to no productivity lost while working from home. A recent study showed that only  1% of productivity was lost when people worked from home. However, every company is different, so it is a good idea to monitor productivity to make sure there is no negative impact. Remote work also helps to expose underperformers faster, and management will have even more pressure to fix this type of issue when it arises.
  Even though there are many benefits of a work-from-home policy, there are also several challenges that leadership needs to be aware of in order to address and mitigate their impact.
  Communications and quality of meetings: An essential aspect of a manager's job is communication. Whether we like it or not, meetings are an important part of our daily interactions and work. It is well documented that  in-person meetings are more effective than online meetings. There is an impact on people's cognitive load caused by online communications. The same goes for creative teams; collaboration under the same roof is more effective than online collaboration.
  The flow of communications: Communication is a critical aspect of knowledge work and services businesses. Remote work tends to increase asynchronous communication. For a lot of companies, the flow of communication needs to be synchronous and needs to happen fast to avoid bottlenecks and wait times for internal and external teams. Remote team communication tends to be more mechanical, and informal conversations happen with less frequency.
  Professional development: People who work remotely  have reported having more challenges with their career and professional development. A lot of professional development opportunities happen in the hallways of the office, impromptu social gatherings and meetings. These social situations are almost nonexistent for remote workers. Distance also decreases healthy competition among teammates.
  Productivity and boundaries between work and personal life: Work-from-home employees have a lot more nonwork related distractions (kids, pets, family, etc.). A lot of times, they find it hard to balance work and their personal life. Also, they might not have the right setup to work from home, which could impact their productivity.
  Increase of inequality: Working from home can accentuate inequality because people who are better off have better conditions in their houses, e.g., a private office. It will give them an upper hand in being more effective and productive.
  Organizational learning: Learning happens in both formal and informal ways. Remote workers tend to have little chances of overhearing an interesting conversion, having a discussion in the hallway or just making an impromptu comment that sparks a thoughtful discussion.
  Scaling: A distributed workforce is difficult to scale. Big companies have reportedly had a  hard time transitioning to a remote workplace.
  Companies should look to amplify the benefits and minimize the challenges of moving to a distributed workforce. The pandemic should not be the main reason companies implement these types of policies in the long term. Organizations should opt for this approach because they believe it will make them more competitive, efficient and better.

note:
“具有噪声的应用程序的基于层次密度的空间集群”(多么冗长的…)。HDBSCAN是我的首选集群算法之一。我觉得这是每个人都应该包括在他们的数据科学工具箱中的一种方法。
在我之前的博客文章中，我已经写过这方面的内容，在那里我试图尽可能深入地解释HDBSCAN。这一次，我采取了相反的方法：我将尽可能简洁地解释HDBSCAN和基于密度的集群的主要思想。
我认为(也希望)这本关于HDBSCAN的入门读物对数据科学的初学者和新手会更友好。
即使提供了正确的簇数，K-Means显然也会给出不好的结果。我们上面确定的一些群集被分成两个或多个群集。另一方面，HDBSCAN为我们提供了预期的集群。
与K-均值不同，基于密度的方法即使在数据不干净且群集形状怪异的情况下也能很好地工作。
HDBSCAN是如何做到这一点的？在较高级别上，我们可以将基于密度的群集过程简化为以下步骤：
我们需要一些方法来估计某些点附近的密度。一种常见的方法是使用“核心距离”。这是一个点到它的第K个最近邻居的距离。
密集区域中的点将具有较小的核心距离，而较稀疏区域中的点将具有较大的核心距离。核心距离是使这些方法“基于密度”的原因。
在给定核心距离的情况下，我们可以通过求密度的倒数来推导出密度的估计值。通过这些估计，我们可以了解密度景观是什么样子。
如果我们绘制这些密度图，我们可以看到这种密度景观的山脉与不同的数据簇相对应。
选择群集的一种方法是选择全局阈值。通过获得密度高于阈值的点，并将这些点分组在一起，我们就得到了我们的聚类。
想象一下海洋上的岛屿，那里的海平面是门槛，不同的岛屿是你的集群。海平面以下的陆地是嘈杂的。随着海平面下降，新的岛屿出现，一些岛屿合并形成更大的岛屿。
这种方法接近于DBSCAN所做的事情。虽然很简单，但这需要我们找到合适的阈值来获得有意义的聚类。
如果将阈值设置得太高，过多的点会被认为是噪波，并且会出现分组不足的情况。如果将其设置得太低，则可能会过度分组这些点，并且所有内容都只是一个簇。
使用全局阈值方法，当簇具有不同的密度时，您可能会遇到困难。如果我们在下面的示例中只使用一个阈值，我们要么对蓝色和黄色群集进行过度分组，要么无法包括整个红色群集。
您可能会认为密度中的每个峰值都应该是一个簇，然而，这并不总是最佳的。
请看下图。在左边，应该有3个簇，在右边，应该有2个簇。
HDBSCAN首先构建一个层次结构，以找出哪些峰值最终会合并在一起，并以什么顺序合并，然后它会问每个集群，是保留这个集群还是将其拆分成它的子集群更好？在上图中，我们应该选择蓝色和黄色区域还是只选择绿色区域？
考虑到密度景观，你可以认为每座山都是一簇。我们必须决定两座山峰是否是同一座山的一部分。是有两座山，还是只有一座山有两个山峰？
要做出选择，我们要看哪一个更“坚持”。我们看到的山峰是更集中还是更分散？群集稳定性(持久性)由层次图中不同颜色区域的面积表示。我们用星团稳定性来回答我们的山脉问题。
当两个山峰实际上是两座山时，两个山峰的体积之和大于它们底部的体积。当山峰只是一座山的特征时，底部的体积就会大于山峰体积的总和。
使用此启发式方法，HDBSCAN能够决定是否将集群细分为其子集群。通过这样做，它会自动选择要提取的群集。
我们根据核心距离估计密度，并形成密度景观(是什么使这些以密度为基础)。
我们可以使用全球阈值将海平面设置为并识别岛屿(DBSCAN)
我们可以试试看，这是几座山还是一座山多峰？(HDBSCAN)。
我希望这能给您提供DBSCAN/HDBSCAN如何工作的依据，以及是什么使这些方法“基于密度”。光学或DEBACL等其他方法使用类似的概念，但它们选择区域的方式不同。
如果您想更多地了解HDBSCAN的统计动机、如何将点组合在一起的实现细节，或者HDBSCAN如何构建层次结构，您可以查看博客帖子，我将在那里进行更详细的介绍。
技术说明：此处绘制的估计密度不只是1/core_Distance。我不得不对数据进行一些转换，使其在视觉上更具吸引力。
note_en:
“ Hierarchical Density-based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise” (What a mouthful…),  HDBSCAN, is one of my go-to clustering algorithms. It’s a method that I feel everyone should include in their data science toolbox.
 I’ve written about this in  my previous blog post, where I try to explain HDBSCAN in as much depth as I could. This time I am taking the opposite approach: I will try to explain the main ideas HDBSCAN and density-based clustering  as succinctly as I can.
 I think (and I hope) that this primer on HDBSCAN would be friendlier for beginners and new-comers in data science.
      Even when provided with the correct number of clusters, K-means clearly gives bad results. Some of the clusters we identified above are separated into two or more clusters. HDBSCAN, on the other hand, gives us the expected clusters.
  Unlike K-means, density-based methods work well even when the data isn’t clean and the clusters are weirdly shaped.
  How does HDBSCAN do this? At a high level, we can simplify the process of density-based clustering into these steps:
   We need some method to estimate the density around certain points. One common way to do this is by using  “core distance.” This is the distance of a point to its K-th nearest neighbor.
  Points in denser regions would have smaller core distances while points in sparser regions would have larger core distances.  Core distance is what makes these methods “density-based”.
  Given the core distances, we can derive an estimate of the density by getting the inverse of it. With these estimates, we can get an idea of what the  density landscape looks like.
  If we plot these densities, we can see that the mountains of this density landscape correspond to the different clusters of the data.
  One way to select clusters is to pick a global threshold. By getting the points with densities above the threshold, and grouping these points together, we get our clusters.
   Imagine islands on the ocean, where the sea level is the threshold and the different islands are your clusters. The land below the sea level is noise. As the sea level goes down, new islands appear and some islands combine to form bigger islands.
  This approach is close to what DBSCAN does. Although simple,  this requires us to find the proper threshold to get meaningful clusters.
 If you set the threshold too high, too many points are considered noise and you have under grouping. If you set it too low, you might over group the points, and everything is just one cluster.
  With the global threshold method, you might have a hard time when the clusters have varying densities. If we use just one threshold in the example below, we either over-group the blue and yellow clusters or we fail to include the entire red cluster.
  You might be tempted to think that each peak in the density should be one cluster, however, this will not always be optimal.
 Look at the image below. On the left, there should be 3 clusters, and on the right, there should be 2 clusters.
  HDBSCAN first builds a hierarchy to figure out which peaks end up merging together and in what order, and then for each cluster it asks,  is it better to keep this cluster or split it up into its subclusters? In the image above, should we pick the  blue and yellow regions or the  green region only?
 Given the density landscape, you can think of each mountain is one cluster. We have to decide whether or not two peaks are part of the same mountain.  Are there two mountains, or just one mountain with two peaks?
      To choose, we look at which one “persists” more. Do we see the peaks more together or apart? Cluster stability (persistence) is represented by the areas of the different colored regions in the hierarchy plot. We use cluster stability to answer our mountain question.
 When the two peaks are actually two mountains, the sum of the volume of the two peaks is larger than the volume of their base. When the peaks are just features of a single mountain, then the volume of the base would be larger than the sum of the peaks’ volume.
 Using this heuristic, HDBSCAN is able to decide whether or not to subdivide a cluster to its subclusters. By doing so, it automatically chooses which clusters to extract.
  We estimate densities based on core distances and form  the density landscape (what makes these density-based)
 We can use a global threshold to  set the sea level at and identify the islands (DBSCAN)
 We can try to decide,  are these several mountains or one mountain with multiple peaks? (HDBSCAN)
 I hope this gives you the gist how DBSCAN/HDBSCAN works and what makes these methods “density based”. Other methods such as OPTICS or DeBaCl use similar concepts but differ in the way they choose the regions.
 If you want to know more about the statistical motivation for HDBSCAN, implementation details of how points are combined together, or how HDBSCAN builds the hierarchy you can  check out blog post where I go into much more detail.
 Technical Note: The estimated densities plotted here isn’t just  1 / core_distance. I had to apply some transformation to the data to make it more visually appealing.
title: HDBSCAN和基于密度的群集的简单介绍
title_en: A gentle introduction to HDBSCAN and density-based clustering
